summary: My family moved to Jacksonville, Florida from Flint, Michigan in 1870 when I was very young. We initially lived in South Jacksonville with the family of ex-Governor Harrison Reed. Jacksonville grew rapidly during my father's time at Barnett Bank, and I witnessed events like the Great Fire of 1901 from the south side of the St. Johns River. Over the years, I saw the city develop, including the laying out of Gibson Street near our home.

My family originally came from Flint, Michigan, where my father was part of the fourth generation of Gibsons settled there. We left Flint in 1870 when my father was ordered to Florida for his health. Though we were considered Yankees in Florida, our roots in Flint were strong and part of our family identity.

Harrison Reed was the first Territorial Governor of Florida and a fellow Yankee from Milwaukee, Wisconsin. My family boarded with his family in South Jacksonville when we first arrived. His son, Harrison Reed Jr., and I were close playmates as children. Governor Reed was also brother to Mrs. Alexander Mitchell, linking our families socially.

Milwaukee, Wisconsin, was the original home of Governor Harrison Reed and Mrs. Alexander Mitchell, who were siblings. Their connection to Milwaukee influenced their lives in Florida, as they brought some of their Midwestern culture and ties with them. The Mitchell family, including David Mitchell, had roots there, and family matters such as divorces were discussed in connection to Milwaukee.

My father secured a position as a bookkeeper at Barnett Bank shortly after we arrived in Jacksonville. He was the third man employed there, working alongside B. Barnett and Mr. Cooper. The bank was located at Forsyth and Main Street and grew significantly over the years, employing hundreds by the time of my father's death in 1911, when he was assistant cashier.

Forsyth Street was the location of the Barnett Bank where my father worked. The bank was on the corner of Forsyth and Main Street, a central spot in Jacksonville. This area was part of the city's commercial heart during my youth.

Main Street, known also as Duval Street, intersected with Forsyth Street where the Barnett Bank was located. This intersection was a key part of Jacksonville's downtown area. I remember it as the place where my father worked and where the bank grew from a small firm to a major institution.

Alexander Mitchell was my great-grandfather, and his name was given to the son of David Mitchell and Kittie Parrott. The young Alexander was born in 1900 and was cared for by my mother at times. He later worked in the bond department of the Barnett Bank and inherited a residence on A. Johns Avenue from his grandmother, Mrs. Lint Parrett.

Villa Alexandria was the large, rambling three-story home owned by Mrs. Alexander Mitchell on A. Johns Avenue. I often stayed there as a child and was fascinated by its beautiful French, Japanese, and antique rooms. The house overlooked the St. Johns River and was filled with fine furnishings and European paintings, reflecting Mrs. Mitchell's cultured and artistic tastes.

A. Johns Avenue was where Mrs. Alexander Mitchell's Villa Alexandria was located, farther out on the south side of Jacksonville. It was also the site of the residence left to Alexander Mitchell by his grandmother, Mrs. Lint Parrett. This avenue was part of the Jacksonville suburb of Avondale and represented a refined residential area.

The St. Johns River was a central feature of life in Jacksonville and South Jacksonville. Our family property extended to the river's edge, and I remember crossing it by rowboat daily. From the roof of our house, I could watch the Great Fire of 1901 across the river, including the burning of the Catholic Church. The river was also the site of early ferryboats like the Armington and Mechanic, which connected the two cities.

David Mitchell was the grandson of Mrs. Alexander Mitchell and came to South Jacksonville around 1893 as a young boy. He was known for his spoiled and wild behavior, including riding polo ponies and gambling. After a serious polo accident left him incapacitated, his wife Kittie Parrott eventually divorced him. Despite his troubles, he was devoted to his grandmother and fathered Alexander Mitchell.

Kittie Parrott, originally Kittie Sutton, was adopted by Mr. Parrott and married David Mitchell. She was determined to marry the richest man in Jacksonville and set her sights on David. After his accident, she refused to live with him and later divorced him, causing local scandal before eventually remarrying and settling in West Palm Beach.
cues: later life in West Palm Beach

summary: The Florida East Coast Railway was significant in Jacksonville's development and connected the city to other regions. The railway owned the Mechanic ferryboat, which carried trains and passengers across the St. Johns River to the south side station. Kittie Parrott's father was an important official in this railway, influencing family dynamics during the Mitchell divorce.

West Palm Beach, Florida became the later home of Kittie Parrott after her third marriage. After her scandalous years in Jacksonville, she moved there and achieved some respectability. It was far from the scenes of her earlier life with David Mitchell and the local scandals.

The Barnett Bank bond department employed Alexander Mitchell, the son of David Mitchell and Kittie Parrott. He was quite young when his parents separated and likely knew little of his extended family. His employment there continued the family's long association with Barnett Bank.

Mrs. Lint Parrett was very fond of Alexander Mitchell and left him her beautiful residence on A. Johns Avenue in Avondale. She was connected to Kittie Parrott by marriage and played a significant role in Alexander's life after his parents separated. Her affection and legacy provided stability for him.

I attended the Bradford Institute, a private school in Jacksonville operated by Mrs. Drow Williams under the Episcopal Church. To get there from the south side, I would take the ferryboat Armington across the St. Johns River. The school was an important part of my early education.

The Episcopal Church was involved in education in Jacksonville through its auspices over the Bradford Institute, where I studied. The church's support helped maintain the private school run by Mrs. Drow Williams. It was part of the community fabric during my youth.

The Flagler System owned the ferryboat Mechanic, which operated on the St. Johns River connecting Jacksonville to the south side railway station. This system was important for freight and passenger transport and was part of the infrastructure development during my youth. The ferry linked the railway trains at Hogan Street to the Florida East Coast Railway South Side Station.

Hogan Street was the location where the Florida East Coast Railway trains arrived on the north side of the river. From there, the Mechanic ferryboat carried freight and passengers across the St. Johns River to the south side station. This area was a key transportation hub in Jacksonville during my youth.

entity: Florida East Coast Railway South Side Station
summary: The Florida East Coast Railway South Side Station was connected to Jacksonville by the Mechanic ferryboat, which transported trains and passengers across the St. Johns River. This station was part of the expanding railway network in the late 19th and early 20th centuries. It helped link the south side of Jacksonville with the rest of the city and region.

I vividly remember the Great Fire of 1901, which started around noon on May 3rd. From the roof of our house on the south side of the river, my siblings and I watched the flames and smoke engulf much of Jacksonville, including the Catholic Church. The fire destroyed many homes and businesses, and our family took in nineteen guests that night. My father stayed at the Barnett Bank until early the next morning, as the bank narrowly escaped the fire.

During the Great Fire of 1901, the Catholic Church in Jacksonville caught fire after a burning brand was carried by the wind. From our vantage point on the south side of the St. Johns River, we watched as the church was rapidly consumed by flames. It was one of the many significant buildings lost in that devastating fire.

Harrison Reed, the son of Governor Harrison Reed, worked for Lite Hubbard Hardware Co. after finishing school. This was his first job before moving on to a position with Standard Oil. His employment there marked the early part of his career in Jacksonville.

Harrison Reed later worked for Standard Oil in Jacksonville, initially managing their warehouse. As the company grew, he was promoted until he became the Florida manager. His career with Standard Oil marked a significant advancement from his earlier work at Lite Hubbard Hardware Co.

San Jose was a neighborhood in Jacksonville where my family lived after purchasing land and building a house near our original boarding place with the Reeds. The street Gibson Street was laid out through our property in 1886. It was part of the expanding residential areas on the south side of the city.
All Saints Chapel is not directly mentioned in my recollections, so I have no specific memories or experiences related to it during my time in Jacksonville.

The yellow fever epidemic of 1888 is not something I personally recall or mention in my experiences. It may have occurred during my youth, but it did not figure prominently in my family's story as I remember it.

The Armington was the first ferryboat operating on the St. Johns River around 1894 or 1896. I used it to cross from the south side to Jacksonville to attend the Bradford Institute. It was a key part of daily life before the bridge was built, connecting the two sides of the river.

The Mechanic was the second ferryboat on the St. Johns River, owned by the Flagler System. It carried trains and passengers from Hogan Street to the Florida East Coast Railway South Side Station. This ferry was important for transportation before the bridge was completed.
